I get that he won't see the kind of money we are paying him at those clubs but he propably won't see even a bench before January and had he gone to be number 1 he'd had half decent shot of making the World Cup squad. Akanji is to Inter should be done before the deadline. Gundogan has been linked with moves to Gala all summer. I'm honestly fine with him staying as a locker room presence but his appearances should propably be limited to Carabao Cup games. Savinho looks like he is staying Kalvin Phillips wasn't given a squad number afaik and is contracted to the club until 2028 and I've not heard any reputable links with his name involved. Isolated it's not a bad window for us but when you look at what Arsenal, Liverpool and even Spurs have done this window I feel like we are well and truly behind. I'm positive about the signings and I don't even really hate the Donnarumma deal but we've had months to facilitate players leaving the club and if things remain as is there is half a starting eleven of players not even making the match day squad in a World Cup year.
label: Analysis

text: Imagine being Arsenal, having one of the best transfer windows in your club's history, fixing all the holes in your squad. Getting a top striker, a top LW, a top DM, and fixing your depth issues, only to look over at what Liverpool is doing and realize that you're probably not winning the title this year!
label: Hot take
notes: Arsenal half is specific (named positions upgraded) but the comparative conclusion rests on zero actual Liverpool signings named - real detail doesn't support the real conclusion.

text: First it was Chelsea blowing the doors of financial fair play. Then City took the piss out of everybody. Now Chelsea can't print money fast enough to buy players, and Liverpool finds a spare half a fucking billion hiding in the couch cushions. I've given up on the idea of Arsenal building a team that can consistently win silverware, as Wenger was building the club to be. Yes, we may have missed our moment, yet again, by not having this transfer window last summer, but winning the league is no longer about nurturing talent, building teams, and investing in the future. It's become a splash-and-dash league like US sports leagues. I'll bet good money that TV timeouts and even shitter VAR will happen within the next five years. I just want to watch enjoyable football without the refs stealing the spotlight every weekend. Sometimes Arsenal give me that but I find myself spending more and more time watching Bundesliga 2 and the Championship every season. It's fun and doesn't require a club spending the GDP of a small nation to have a chance of winning a trophy.
label: Hot take
notes: Mixed-mode (edge case 3): opens with real financial framing but the real conclusion drifts into broad, unargued despair claims (giving up on Arsenal, predicting US-style timeouts) - vague gestures rather than named mechanisms.

text: This is the first transfer window I'm just annoyed and not mildly amused at the madness. There are clubs sending players to their ~~feeder~~ partner teams to help take on the wages, clubs spending obscene transfer fees from money generated by selling women's teams and hotels to themselves for profit, and all the while there are clubs having to sell their academy graduates just to balance the books just to fit some absurd model where being the owner in a 'blind trust' means you don't have any control over the club but you can still come on the pitch and remonstrate with your staff who you don't manage because you have no influence but still have enough influence to come onto the pitch. It's just a joke. Anyway, ready for Sheffield Wednesday to spend 5 million on a centre forward.
label: Analysis
notes: Names several concrete, checkable financial mechanisms (feeder/partner loan parking, asset sales to inflate FFP profit, academy sales to balance books) rather than vague gestures - closer to the Chelsea/Strasbourg precedent than to the despair-rant pattern above.

text: Some thoughts about today's match: We still struggle to create open play chances even if it was our b team. Lautaro and Julian, especially Julian, are clearly out of form and it's very noticeable. I though Almada and barco were great as subs and barco should play more because his energy, passing and overall understanding of the game are incredible. Otamendi looks way past it and any decent attacker will expose him, fortunately he is not a starter. I thought Senesi was decent and had a couple of good recoveries. Messi was not very good today outside of the goal that was honestly more on the keeper than on him. Nico Paz had a couple of good plays but I still expect more from him, it also feels like he is another player out of form (he is coming from an injury). The biggest problem right now is that we haven't replaced di María and we desperately need a player that can take on opponents and stretch defenses because when we can't play through the middle we don't look really good. Simeone was fine as a wing back but he doesn't have the technicall ability to dribble past defenders

text: Pretty crazy the amount of disrespect people give to non-Messi SA teams. Argentina has not looked as good as their scorelines suggest at a glance. Messi is fantastic, but he hasn't faced top opposition. Brazil started off looking very average, but are now looking much more complete than Argentina. The last three games between colombia and Argentina were 1-1 in Argentina, 2-1 for Colombia in Colombia, and 1-0 for Argentina in Extra Time against a Muñoz-less Colombia. People were convinced that Ronaldo would be facing Messi in the Quarters, but Colombia were clearly the better team and sent Portugal over to be eliminated by Spain on the other side. This bracket is not clear-cut for Argentina by any means. Not to mention England, but they haven't looked convincing either and should absolutely have rightfully lost against Ghana. But the other side is absolutely clear cut. France is going to win the whole thing
label: Analysis
notes: Heavily evidenced (multiple specific scorelines) with a bare unsupported prediction tacked on the end - kept Analysis since the bulk of the comment is load-bearing detail.
